comments.author: @franktoledo6342
comments.text: Weed causes suicidal thoughts which turns to reality. I think the Dutch need to protect the healthy people the unhealthy destroy them self.

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: The Netherlands operates on a harm reduction model. They do not fully legalize cannabis but tolerate soft drugs under strict regulations to separate them from the hard drug market. This reflects a pragmatic approach to social policy, prioritizing long-term stability and public health control in the same structured way they handle national retirement planning.

comments.author: @franktoledo6342
comments.text: Is the water 💦 extremely safe to drink. I would say this to the Dutch only the stupidity in the world legalized the devil weed. I mean completely stupid.

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: Yes, Dutch tap water is purified without chlorine and ranks among the safest and highest quality globally. Regarding your second point, cannabis is heavily regulated and tolerated, not fully legalized. The Dutch system focuses on strict management and long-term societal structuring whether it is water infrastructure, public health, or retirement planning rather than outright bans.

comments.author: @AmazingDiscoveriesTopUS
comments.text: The Netherlands proves money isn't everything... so why do so many still chase it?

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: That is a deep point! While wealth is chased, many now prioritize quality of life for retirement planning. Do you believe the Dutch balance between happiness and wealth is the key to living well?

comments.author: @MarkA.Dufrene
comments.text:
Good article.  Two questions:
1.  I would like to know how do they find the energy to move all that water around?  What is the source of their electricity?  
2.  How do they manage the sewage when there is so much water and so little land mass?  Do they have a problem with waste water?  What do they do with it?  Do they pump it inland to treatment facilities?  Do they have a problem with foul smells in the air?

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: That is a deep point! While wealth is chased, many now prioritize quality of life for retirement planning. Do you believe the Dutch balance between happiness and wealth is the key to living well?

comments.author: @DiscoverGlobePlacesTop10_NL158
comments.text: The Netherlands is one of the happiest coutries on Earth,yet many young people can't afford a home.Is happiness really about money ?

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: You hit the nail on the head! Happiness is complex. Even with housing challenges, many still view this region for retirement planning. In your opinion, is money the biggest factor for happiness?

comments.author: @lancewilliams5726
comments.text: I really enjoyed visiting the Netherlands; the slow pace really felt good😎

replies.author: @MarkA.Dufrene
replies.text: When people ask me my age I tell them, 70 with high mileage.  Trying to keep up with American way of life you will have high mileage at midlife.  Now that I’m 70 and retired I struggle with trying to relax.  I have pushed for so many years I feel like I’m being irresponsible if I’m not producing something in a productive manner.  😮😊

replies.author: @EuropeExploresAmerica
replies.text: The video makes it look so chill, especially with everyone cycling everywhere. It seems like a great place to unwind and just enjoy the moment.

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: The relaxed pace definitely stands out. It actually makes the Netherlands a solid option to consider for your retirement planning. Did you notice any of those bizarre local rules during your visit?

comments.author: @istvanhutter5109
comments.text: I like this Dutch spirit! If I lived there! I looked at a living boat to buy and live in the Netherlands. But it was very expensive. (about 10 meter-long was cost more than 10 million in my currency. Which country is a real paradise? Most of them are honest at least. (the Dutch)

replies.author: @TravelToFacts
replies.text: If you had the chance, would you still want to live on a boat in the Netherlands?

replies.author: @istvanhutter5109
replies.text: @EuropeExploresAmerica Exactly! This everlasting fight against the sea is expensive. This is why life and the flats in the Netherlands are so expensive. My dream is unreachable. I had better see it. It was impossible to live in Michigan, too. Authorities don't allow. Winters are long, too and colder. They hate men from Eastern Europe. They like only the Africans and the Asians.

replies.author: @EuropeExploresAmerica
replies.text: Yeah, the housing crisis there is no joke, as the video mentioned. Houseboats are super cool but so pricey! Their honesty is definitely refreshing though.

replies.author: @istvanhutter5109
replies.text: @Outland_US I don't want to save money, coz I don't have any. Without money, the man has to stay wherever he is. I'm an Eastern European pensioner. I was a worker for my whole life. This means I'm not rich. I never earned much money. Only it was enough to go to work the next day. Relocating is a dream only. I tried it three times (and I'll try it again) and I realised that without much money, it's impossible. I was a car mechanic and a professional driver. Never had bad habits. And no money! Isn't it interesting?

replies.author: @istvanhutter5109
replies.text: ​@Outland_US , those countries are too hot, too sunny. I hate the heat. I don't want to burn in the sun rays! The Netherlands, with its weather, is good.

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: Honestly, for budget friendly retirement planning by the water, Portugal or parts of Spain might fit better than the Netherlands. No country's perfect, but a few come close!

replies.author: @Outland_US
replies.text: Living on a houseboat in the Netherlands requires significant capital. Mooring fees, ongoing maintenance, and high housing demand drive up these prices significantly. Factoring these exact living expenses into your relocation or retirement planning is essential before making a move.
